practice.name: Problem 15 (2025 AMC 8)
practice.statement:
Kei draws a $6 \times 6$ grid. He colors $13$ of the unit squares silver and the remaining squares gold. Kei then folds the grid in half vertically, forming pairs of overlapping unit squares. Let $m$ and $M$ be the least and greatest possible number of gold-on-gold pairs, respectively. What is $m + M$? [See figure on AoPS]

$\textbf{(A)}\ 12 \qquad \textbf{(B)}\ 14 \qquad \textbf{(C)}\ 16 \qquad \textbf{(D)}\ 18 \qquad \textbf{(E)}\ 20$

practice.name: Problem 7 (2024 AMC 8)
practice.statement:
A $3 \times 7$ rectangle is covered without overlap by $3$ shapes of tiles: $2 \times 2$, $1 \times 4$, and $1 \times 1$, shown below. What is the minimum possible number of $1 \times 1$ tiles used? [See figure on AoPS]

$\textbf{(A)}\ 1 \qquad \textbf{(B)}\ 2 \qquad \textbf{(C)}\ 3 \qquad \textbf{(D)}\ 4 \qquad \textbf{(E)}\ 5$

practice.name: Problem 16 (2024 AMC 8)
practice.statement:
Minh enters the numbers $1$ through $81$ into the cells of a $9 \times 9$ grid in some order. She calculates the product of the numbers in each row and column. What is the least number of these products that could be a multiple of $3$?

$\textbf{(A)}\ 8 \qquad \textbf{(B)}\ 9 \qquad \textbf{(C)}\ 10 \qquad \textbf{(D)}\ 11 \qquad \textbf{(E)}\ 12$

practice.name: Problem 18 (2023 AMC 8)
practice.statement:
Greta Grasshopper sits on a long line of lily pads in a pond. From any lily pad, Greta can jump $5$ pads to the right or $3$ pads to the left. What is the fewest number of jumps Greta must make to reach the lily pad located $2023$ pads to the right of her starting position?

$\textbf{(A)}\ 405 \qquad \textbf{(B)}\ 407 \qquad \textbf{(C)}\ 409 \qquad \textbf{(D)}\ 411 \qquad \textbf{(E)}\ 413$

practice.name: Problem 18 (2002 AMC 10A)
practice.statement:
A $3 \times 3 \times 3$ cube is formed by gluing together $27$ standard cubical dice. (On a standard die, the sum of the numbers on any pair of opposite faces is $7$.) The smallest possible sum of all the numbers showing on the surface of the $3 \times 3 \times 3$ cube is

$\textbf{(A)}\ 60 \qquad \textbf{(B)}\ 72 \qquad \textbf{(C)}\ 84 \qquad \textbf{(D)}\ 90 \qquad \textbf{(E)}\ 96$
